solves.approach: Brute Force Linear Scan with Digit Validation
solves.essence: For each number 1 to n, check if all digits are rotatable and at least one digit changes after rotation.
solves.time_complexity: O(n * log10(n)) — for each of the n numbers, we process each digit (log10(i) digits per number)
solves.title: Rotated Digits

solves.approach: Brute Force Linear Scan with Digit Validation
solves.essence: Iterate every number up to n, checking each digit for rotation validity and change requirement.
solves.time_complexity: O(n * d) — where d is the number of digits (log10(n)), so effectively O(n log n) but since d ≤ 5 for n ≤ 10000, it's practically O(n).
solves.title: Rotated Digits

solves.time_complexity: O(n log n) — outer loop runs n times, and for each number we extract O(log n) digits
solves.title: Rotated Digits

solves.essence: Concatenate s with itself and check if goal is a substring, covering all rotations in one shot.
solves.time_complexity: O(n) — Python's 'in' and C++'s find() use optimized substring search (KMP-like internally); n is the length of s.
solves.title: Rotate String

solves.time_complexity: O(n) — where n is the length of s; modern string search (KMP-based internally) runs in O(n), though naive find is O(n²) in worst case

solves.essence: Check if goal is a substring of s+s to detect any rotation in O(n) time.
solves.time_complexity: O(n) — length check is O(1), string concatenation is O(n), and Python's 'in' operator uses optimized substring search (roughly O(n))
solves.title: Rotate String

solves.essence: Check if goal is a substring of s+s to detect all possible rotations at once
solves.time_complexity: O(n) — Python's 'in' operator uses optimized substring search (similar to KMP/Boyer-Moore) on a string of length 2n
solves.title: Rotate String

solves.essence: Merge both sorted arrays, sort the result, then pick the middle element(s) as the median.
solves.time_complexity: O((m+n) log(m+n)) — dominated by sorting the merged array of size m+n; copying is O(m+n)
solves.title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays

solves.approach: Hash Table / Boolean Tracking with Bit Marking
solves.essence: Use three 9x9 boolean arrays to track digit occurrences in rows, columns, and 3x3 boxes simultaneously in a single pass.
solves.time_complexity: O(1) — The board is always 9x9 = 81 cells, so the iteration is constant regardless of input.
solves.title: Valid Sudoku

solves.essence: Directly compute zigzag output by iterating row by row and calculating diagonal character positions mathematically.
solves.time_complexity: O(n) — each character in the string is visited exactly once across all row iterations.
solves.title: Zigzag Conversion

solves.essence: Recursively build valid parentheses by tracking remaining open/close counts and only placing brackets when they maintain validity.
solves.time_complexity: O(4^n / sqrt(n)) — the number of valid combinations is the nth Catalan number C(n) = (2n choose n)/(n+1), and each string has length 2n, so total work is O(n * Catalan(n)) ≈ O(4^n / sqrt(n)).
solves.title: Generate Parentheses

solves.approach: Fast Exponentiation (Binary Exponentiation / Recursive Divide and Conquer)
solves.essence: Halve the exponent recursively to compute x^n in O(log n) by squaring intermediate results.
solves.time_complexity: O(log n) — each recursive call halves the exponent, resulting in log2(n) levels of recursion
solves.title: Pow(x, n)

solves.approach: Fast Exponentiation (Binary Exponentiation / Recursive Divide and Conquer)
solves.essence: Halve the exponent recursively to compute power in O(log n) by squaring intermediate results.
solves.time_complexity: O(log n) — each recursive call halves the exponent, resulting in log2(n) levels of recursion
solves.title: Pow(x, n)

solves.essence: Use a single 1D DP array to track minimum path sums row by row, reusing space by updating in-place.
solves.time_complexity: O(m*n) — every cell in the grid is visited exactly once
solves.title: Minimum Path Sum

solves.essence: Reverse only the second half of the number and compare with the first half to avoid overflow and extra space.
solves.time_complexity: O(log10(n)) — we only iterate through half the digits of the number
solves.title: Palindrome Number

solves.approach: Circular Linked List with Pointer Manipulation
solves.essence: Connect tail to head forming a circle, then break at the new tail position after (len - k) steps.
solves.time_complexity: O(n) — single pass to find length, then at most one more pass to find the new tail
solves.title: Rotate List

solves.essence: Rotate matrix 90° clockwise by transposing across main diagonal then reversing each row.
solves.time_complexity: O(n²) — every element is visited once during transpose (n²/2 swaps) and once during row reversal (n²/2 swaps), totaling O(n²).
solves.title: Rotate Image

solves.essence: Rotate matrix 90° clockwise by transposing across main diagonal then reversing each row.
solves.time_complexity: O(n²) — every element is visited once during transpose (n²/2 swaps) and once during row reversal (n²/2 swaps), totaling O(n²).
solves.title: Rotate Image

solves.essence: Solution analysis failed
solves.time_complexity: Unknown
solves.title: Rotating the Box

solves.approach: Prefix Maximum + Suffix Minimum with Right-to-Left DP
solves.essence: Use prefix max and suffix min arrays to determine reachable maximum values via valid jumps in a single right-to-left pass.
solves.time_complexity: O(n) — single pass to build prefix max array and single right-to-left pass for the answer
solves.title: Jump Game IX

solves.approach: BFS with Prime Teleportation via Smallest Prime Factor Sieve
solves.essence: Use BFS on index graph where edges include adjacent steps and prime-grouped teleportations, marking used prime channels to avoid redundant traversal.
solves.time_complexity: O(MAXV + n * log(maxVal)) — SPF sieve is O(MAXV log log MAXV), factorization per element is O(log maxVal), BFS visits each index and each prime channel at most once so total teleportation work is O(n).
solves.title: Minimum Jumps to Reach End via Prime Teleportation
